allResults.title: Lonnie Smith Gets Deked
allResults.concept: Watch the ball, not the fielders — dekes only work if you take your eyes off the play

allResults.title: Cover Third on a Steal From Second
allResults.concept: On steals of third, the third baseman covers the bag — straddle it, catch the throw, and sweep the tag down on the runner's feet.

allResults.title: 3-1 Count — Pitcher Must Throw a Strike
allResults.concept: 3-1 counts are great for stealing — the pitcher must throw a fastball strike

allResults.title: Chopper Over the Pitcher — Score?
allResults.concept: On high choppers that take the fielder away from you, be aggressive — score if you can

allResults.title: Is This Steal Worth It? High School Math
allResults.concept: Break-even is the MINIMUM, not the target — adjust your steal decision for the specific catcher's arm, not just your season average

allResults.title: Steal or Hit and Run?
allResults.concept: With your fastest runner and a slow pitcher, the straight steal is cleaner than a hit and run

allResults.title: Back-Pick on a Runner Who Rounds the Bag
allResults.concept: Great catchers stay alert after every play — when a runner overslides a base, a quick throw can catch him off the bag

allResults.title: Infield Fly Rule
allResults.concept: Infield fly rule: batter is out, ball is live, runners advance at own risk — prevents trick double plays

allResults.title: Designated Hitter Rule
allResults.concept: The designated hitter (DH) bats for the pitcher — universal in MLB since 2022

allResults.title: Neighborhood Play at Second Base
allResults.concept: The neighborhood play allows second basemen to be slightly off the bag during double play turns — protecting the fielder from sliding runners

allResults.title: 2-0 Sitting Fastball
allResults.concept: A 2-0 count is a great opportunity. Look for a fastball in YOUR zone — if you get it, swing hard. If not, you're still ahead in the count.

allResults.title: 3-1 Green Light
allResults.concept: A 3-1 green light means look for YOUR pitch and attack it. The pitcher needs a strike, so the fastball is coming — be ready!

allResults.title: Hit and Run — Your Read
allResults.concept: Hit and run: 2B must read the ball — field if in range, cover if not

allResults.title: The 3rd Out Rule
allResults.concept: With less than 2 outs, never make the 3rd out at home on a close play — being out at home kills the inning and all runners behind you

allResults.title: Covering First Base
allResults.concept: Pitchers must cover 1st base on ground balls to the right side — sprint to the bag!

allResults.title: Windup or Stretch?
allResults.concept: Always pitch from the stretch with runners on base — it lets you control the running game

allResults.title: Bases Loaded Jam
allResults.concept: Bases loaded vs a hot hitter — pitch around him carefully, don't walk in the run and don't groove one

allResults.title: Big Lead Comfort Zone
allResults.concept: With a big lead, stay aggressive but don't overthrow — attack the zone and let hitters get themselves out

allResults.title: They Just Took You Deep — Keep Your Cool
allResults.concept: After giving up a homer, reset mentally — short memory, stay with your game plan

allResults.title: Known Base Stealer — Adjust Timing
allResults.concept: Against base stealers, vary your hold time — unpredictable timing ruins their jump

allResults.title: Lefty Advantage — Pickoff Move
allResults.concept: Left-handed pitchers have a natural pickoff advantage — your body faces first base. Use it!

allResults.title: Cover First on the Wild Strikeout
allResults.concept: On a dropped third strike, the pitcher MUST sprint to cover first base — it becomes a footrace between the pitcher and the batter-runner.

allResults.title: Coors Field — Change Your Game Plan
allResults.concept: At altitude (Coors Field), breaking balls lose ~15% movement — adjust your pitch mix to rely more on fastball command and less on spin-based movement

allResults.title: Which Pitch to Expect
allResults.concept: Look fastball first — it's the most common pitch. React to everything else

allResults.title: Runner on Third — Make Contact
allResults.concept: With a runner on 3rd and less than 2 outs, a fly ball is the most productive play

allResults.title: Infield In — Fly or Ground?
allResults.concept: Against a drawn-in infield, fly balls beat ground balls — the runner scores on a sac fly

allResults.title: Sacrifice Bunt — 9-Hole Hitter
allResults.concept: As the weakest hitter, the sacrifice bunt trades your out for a runner in scoring position — a good trade

allResults.title: Squeeze Play with Runner on Third
allResults.concept: On a squeeze play, bunt the ball no matter what — the runner is committed and needs you to make contact

allResults.title: Suicide Squeeze — Bad Pitch
allResults.concept: On a suicide squeeze, bunt the ball no matter where it's thrown — your teammate's safety depends on you

allResults.title: Power Hitter Asked to Bunt
allResults.concept: Execute the coach's sign even if you disagree — trust, teamwork, and discipline win games

allResults.title: Two Strikes — Change Your Swing
allResults.concept: With 2 strikes, choke up and shorten your swing — contact is more important than power

allResults.title: Protecting the Plate with Runners On
allResults.concept: With 2 strikes and runners on, foul off borderline pitches — keep the at-bat alive

allResults.title: Fouling Off — Battle Mode
allResults.concept: Long at-bats wear down pitchers — keep fouling off tough pitches until they make a mistake

allResults.title: Two Strikes — Expand the Zone?
allResults.concept: With 2 strikes, expand your zone slightly — protect the plate without chasing bad pitches

allResults.title: Winning Big — Still Compete
allResults.concept: Compete the same way in every at-bat — don't showboat in a blowout or give up in a loss

allResults.title: Pitcher Hit Your Teammate — Stay Focused
allResults.concept: After a teammate gets hit, the best revenge is a base hit — stay calm and focused

allResults.title: Cold Weather — Adjust for Power
allResults.concept: In cold weather, switch to a line-drive approach — fly balls die but line drives travel in any temperature

allResults.title: Hit and Run — Where Should You Hit It?
allResults.concept: On the hit-and-run, hit the ball on the ground to the right side — the middle infielder covering second leaves a hole

allResults.title: Relay Position
allResults.concept: On relay throws, line up directly between the outfielder and the target base

allResults.title: Bases Empty — Routine Grounder
allResults.concept: With nobody on base, every ground ball goes to first — know where to throw before the ball is hit

allResults.title: Turning Two at the Pivot
allResults.concept: On a double play pivot, catch the ball, touch the bag, and make a quick accurate throw to first — footwork and fast hands are everything.

allResults.title: Ball Up the Middle
allResults.concept: When ranging to your left as a second baseman, backhand the ball, plant hard, and throw across your body — spinning around wastes too much time.

allResults.title: Covering First on the Bunt
allResults.concept: On a bunt play when the first baseman charges, the second baseman must sprint over to cover first base — hesitation means a free base.

allResults.title: Tag Play at Second Base
allResults.concept: On a steal tag play, straddle the bag and sweep the glove tag down in front of the base — let the runner slide right into it.

allResults.title: Holding the Runner at Second
allResults.concept: Hold runners close by faking toward the bag, then hustle back to fielding position before the pitch — balance runner control with defensive positioning.

allResults.title: Hit and Run Coverage
allResults.concept: On a hit-and-run, the second baseman holds position to cover the right side while the shortstop covers second — don't leave your zone empty.

allResults.title: Bunt Play — Cover First!
allResults.concept: On a sacrifice bunt, the second baseman must sprint to cover first base when the first baseman charges — every fielder has a specific bunt responsibility.

allResults.title: Tag at Second on the Steal
allResults.concept: On a steal tag, catch the throw and sweep the glove down in front of the base — let the runner slide into your tag instead of chasing him.

allResults.title: Positioning vs. a Lefty Pull Hitter
allResults.concept: Against left-handed pull hitters, shade toward first base where they're most likely to hit — play the percentages but stay close enough to second for steal and double play coverage.

allResults.title: DP Pivot — Feed to Shortstop
allResults.concept: DP feed to SS: underhand flip close in, overhand throw from deep

allResults.title: The Bunt — Who Takes First?
allResults.concept: On bunt defense: 2B covers first when 1B charges

allResults.title: Relay from Right Field — Who Takes It?
allResults.concept: RF relay: SS is primary relay man, 2B backs up and covers second

allResults.title: First and Third — Your Role
allResults.concept: 1st and 3rd: fake the tag at 2nd, read the runner at 3rd before throwing

allResults.title: Runner Breaks from Third
allResults.concept: On a slow roller from normal depth, the runner from third will beat your throw home — take the sure out at first

allResults.title: Double Play Depth
allResults.concept: Double play depth means a few steps toward 2nd and a step in — shorter path to start the DP

allResults.title: Fast Runner at the Plate — Adjust Depth
allResults.concept: Against fast runners, play a step in — a shorter throw to first compensates for their speed

allResults.title: Relay from Deep Center — Who Goes Where?
allResults.concept: On balls to center/left, the shortstop is the relay man — the second baseman covers second base

allResults.title: Deep in the Hole
allResults.concept: On a deep backhand in the hole, plant hard, set your feet, and make a strong throw across the diamond — this is the shortstop's signature play.

allResults.title: Starting the Double Play
allResults.concept: On a double play feed to second, make a firm chest-high throw the second baseman can handle — accuracy beats raw speed every time.

allResults.title: Covering on the Steal
allResults.concept: On steal coverage, get to the bag early, straddle it to give the catcher a target, and sweep the tag down in front of the base.

allResults.title: Pop Fly in Shallow Left
allResults.concept: Outfielders coming in have priority over infielders going back — running forward with the ball in front of you is always easier than chasing it over your shoulder.

allResults.title: Do-Or-Die With Runner on Third
allResults.concept: On a do-or-die play with a runner scoring from third, charge hard and throw home — the out at first doesn't matter if the winning run scores.

allResults.title: Positioning for a Pull Hitter
allResults.concept: Against pull hitters, shade a few steps toward the side they tend to hit — positioning yourself where the ball is most likely to go is smart defense.

allResults.title: Bare-Hand the Swinging Bunt
allResults.concept: On a dying swinging bunt, charge hard and bare-hand it — the scoop-and-throw in one motion is the only way to beat the runner on a slow roller.

allResults.title: Ranging Deep in the Hole
allResults.concept: On a deep backhand in the hole, plant hard and fire across the diamond — your arm strength and footwork are what make this signature shortstop play possible.

allResults.title: Steal Coverage With a Righty Batting
allResults.concept: With a right-handed batter, the shortstop covers second on a steal because the second baseman needs to protect the right side where a righty is more likely to hit.

allResults.title: Double Play Depth or Normal Depth?
allResults.concept: Double play depth means a few steps toward second base and a step in toward home — shorter distance to the bag and quicker reactions make turning two possible.

allResults.title: Pop Fly in Shallow Left
allResults.concept: Outfielders running in always have priority over infielders going back — the ball is easier to see and catch when it's in front of you, not over your shoulder.

allResults.title: Throwing on the Run — Backhand Side
allResults.concept: When ranging to the backhand side without time to plant, throw sidearm on the run — use your momentum toward first base to power the throw instead of fighting it.

allResults.title: Relay Positioning — Where Do You Line Up?
allResults.concept: Relay positioning: shallow outfield grass, inline with target base

allResults.title: Steal Coverage — Who Has Second?
allResults.concept: Stolen base coverage: SS covers on LHB, 2B covers on RHB

allResults.title: Communication on the Pop-Up
allResults.concept: Shortstop has priority over third base on shared popup zone

allResults.title: The 5-4-3 Double Play — Your Throw
allResults.concept: On the DP pivot, accuracy beats speed — set your feet

allResults.title: Pop Fly Priority
allResults.concept: Center fielder has priority on all fly balls — call it early and loud!

allResults.title: Extra Base Hit to the Gap
allResults.concept: On balls in the gap, use the relay man — two short throws beat one long throw

allResults.title: Routine Fly — Catch It Right
allResults.concept: Catch fly balls above your head with two hands — safe, reliable, and ready to throw

allResults.title: Ball Between Two Outfielders — Who Gets It?
allResults.concept: The center fielder has priority on fly balls between outfielders — he calls it and everyone else defers

allResults.title: Ball Deep to the Warning Track
allResults.concept: On deep fly balls, use an angle route — turn and run to the spot, don't drift back or run straight

allResults.title: Back Up the Play
allResults.concept: Great center fielders are always moving to back up throws, even on routine plays — that's how you prevent disaster.

allResults.title: Relay Throw From the Gap
allResults.concept: On relay plays, the center fielder lines up behind the cutoff man and calls out where to throw — you're the eyes of the relay.

allResults.title: Line Drive Read
allResults.concept: On a hard-hit ball, your first step should always be back — it's easier to come in than go back.

allResults.title: Ball Off the Wall
allResults.concept: On balls off the wall, set up a few feet away from the wall to play the carom cleanly — don't chase the ball to the wall.

allResults.title: Gap Communication
allResults.concept: The center fielder is the captain of the outfield — call for every ball you can reach, loudly and more than once.

allResults.title: Crow-Hop and Throw
allResults.concept: Always hit your cutoff man with a crow-hop throw — it gives your team two chances to make a play instead of one.

allResults.title: Reading the Swing — Bat Angle Predicts Direction
allResults.concept: Read the bat angle at contact — rolled hands and an uppercut on high fastballs produce pull-side fly balls. Shade early for a jump.

allResults.title: Getting Behind the Ball — Never Let It Go Over Your Head
allResults.concept: First step back on every fly ball — it's easier to run forward than to turn and chase a ball over your head

allResults.title: Hit the Cutoff Man
allResults.concept: Always hit the cutoff man — it gives your team the most options and prevents wild throws

allResults.title: Runner Trying to Score from First on a Double
allResults.concept: On doubles off the wall, the relay throw is essential — no arm is strong enough to throw 350 feet accurately

allResults.title: Wind Blowing Out — Play Deeper
allResults.concept: When the wind blows out, play deeper — fly balls carry farther and you need the extra room

allResults.title: Hit the Cutoff
allResults.concept: Always hit the cutoff man — it keeps your throw accurate and gives your team options on where to send the ball.

allResults.title: Two Runners Moving
allResults.concept: With multiple runners moving, always hit the cutoff man — he can see the whole field and redirect to the best play.

allResults.title: Reading Off the Bat
allResults.concept: On fly balls, your first step determines everything — a drop step back gives you time to read the carry and adjust.

allResults.title: Backing Up Third Base
allResults.concept: The left fielder always backs up third base on throws from the catcher — sprint behind the bag, in line with the throw.

allResults.title: Lost in the Sun
allResults.concept: When you lose a ball in the sun, use your glove to shade your eyes and shift your angle — never stare directly into the sun.

allResults.title: Shallow Fly Communication
allResults.concept: An outfielder running in has a better angle than an infielder running out — call for the ball and take charge.

allResults.title: Reading the Wall Carom
allResults.concept: When a carom takes the ball away from you, let a teammate field it and switch to being the relay man — smart positioning beats foot speed.

allResults.title: Hard Carom Off the Wall — Line Drive
allResults.concept: On hard wall caroms, set up 10-15 feet off the wall — let the ball come to you, field it cleanly, and use the cutoff system

allResults.title: Sprint-to-Throw Transition — The Crow Hop
allResults.concept: Round the ball on gap hits — curve your path so your momentum faces the infield when you field it, then crow-hop for a strong, accurate throw

allResults.title: Bunt Defense — Who Covers What?
allResults.concept: Bunt defense is a team play — corners charge, SS covers 3rd, 2B covers 1st, pitcher fields

allResults.title: Scooping a Short-Hop Throw
allResults.concept: First basemen save infielders' errors by scooping short hops — keep your foot on the bag, stretch toward the throw, and get your glove down low.

allResults.title: Cutoff Positioning on a Single to Right
allResults.concept: On throws to home from right field, the first baseman is the cutoff man — line up between the fielder and home plate with your arms raised.

allResults.title: Foul Ball Communication Near First
allResults.concept: On foul balls near your position, call it loudly and early — communication prevents collisions and dropped balls.

allResults.title: First-and-Third Pickoff Play
allResults.concept: In first-and-third pickoff plays, the first baseman must stay on the bag — the throw might come right back to you for a surprise out.

allResults.title: Picking the Ball in the Dirt
allResults.concept: Scooping throws in the dirt is a first baseman's signature skill — keep your foot on the bag, stretch toward the throw, and sweep your glove low along the ground.

allResults.title: The Cutoff — RF to Home
allResults.concept: First base is the cutoff man on throws from right field to home

allResults.title: Scooping the Short-Hop
allResults.concept: Short-hop scoop: keep foot on bag, drop glove to dirt, scoop through

allResults.title: Pickoff Tag — Swipe or Sweep?
allResults.concept: Pickoff tag: swipe at front bag corner, back of glove down

allResults.title: Bunt Defense — Your Crash
allResults.concept: Bunt defense: 1B crashes hard on the pitch, not after contact

allResults.title: Runner on Second — Hold or Play Back?
allResults.concept: Never hold a runner at second base — no strategic value

allResults.title: Relay Position on Extra-Base Hit to Right
allResults.concept: On extra-base hits to right field, the first baseman is the cutoff man between the outfielder and home plate

allResults.title: Bunt With Runner on Second — Where to Throw?
allResults.concept: On sacrifice bunts, take the sure out — don't try to be a hero by getting the lead runner when the throw is risky

allResults.title: 3-1 Putout — Unassisted
allResults.concept: When you field the ball near first base and can reach the bag, take the unassisted putout — no throw means no error

allResults.title: Pop-Up Near the Dugout
allResults.concept: Pop-ups on the first base side belong to the first baseman — call for the ball and watch for the dugout railing

allResults.title: Wide Throw From the Shortstop
allResults.concept: Stretch with your glove foot on the bag to maximize reach — keeping the force play alive is almost always better than coming off the bag to tag

allResults.title: Holding the Runner With a Left-Handed Pitcher
allResults.concept: First baseman and lefty pitcher work as a unit on pickoffs — pre-arranged signals and a visible target make the timing devastating

allResults.title: Backing Up Bases
allResults.concept: Always back up the base where the throw is going — anticipate overthrows

allResults.title: Who Backs Up the Throw?
allResults.concept: Right fielders back up first base on infield grounders — prevent one error from becoming two bases

allResults.title: Runner Rounding Second Hard
allResults.concept: On singles to right with runners advancing, hit the cutoff — he can redirect to the right base

allResults.title: Fly Ball in the Sun
allResults.concept: On fly balls in the sun, use your glove as a sun shield — raise it to block the sun while tracking the ball

allResults.title: Communication Between Outfielders
allResults.concept: On fly balls between fielders, CALL IT loudly — communication prevents collisions and errors

allResults.title: Gun Down at Third
allResults.concept: Right fielders with strong arms can keep runners from going first-to-third — that throw takes away a scoring-position runner.

allResults.title: Stop First-to-Third
allResults.concept: Charge ground ball singles aggressively in right field — every second you save keeps runners from going first-to-third.

allResults.title: Hit the Cutoff Man
allResults.concept: When you can't reach home on the fly, always hit your cutoff man — a good relay is faster than a bad long throw.

allResults.title: Playing the Wall
allResults.concept: Learn how balls carom off the wall — corner hits angle toward center field, so position yourself where the bounce will go, not where the ball hits.

allResults.title: Shallow Fly Communication
allResults.concept: Outfielders have priority over infielders on fly balls because running in is easier than running out with the ball over your shoulder.

allResults.title: Back Up First Base
allResults.concept: The right fielder backs up first base on every infield grounder — it's a routine responsibility that prevents disaster on overthrows.

allResults.title: Charge or Play It Safe
allResults.concept: In close games with two outs, charge the ball aggressively — saving a half-second on your throw can be the difference between an out and a run scoring.

allResults.title: Backing Up First on a Throw From Third
allResults.concept: The right fielder must back up first base on throws from the left side of the infield — long throws from third are the most likely to get past the first baseman.

allResults.title: Cutoff Man or Throw Home Direct
allResults.concept: In pressure situations, hitting the cutoff man is smarter than a hero throw — accurate relay throws beat long, risky heaves to the plate.

allResults.title: Cut Off the Extra Base to the Gap
allResults.concept: On gap hits, the closest outfielder sprints to the ball and hits the cutoff man — urgency prevents extra bases and keeps runners from scoring.

allResults.title: Corner Carom — Ball Off the Right-Field Wall
allResults.concept: Read wall caroms like a billiard ball — the angle of incidence predicts where the ball will kick. Move to where it's going, not where it hit.

allResults.title: Bunt Against the Shift
allResults.concept: Always adjust positioning when the batter shows bunt

allResults.title: Slow Roller — Eat It or Throw?
allResults.concept: If you don't have a clean throw, eat the ball — an error is worse than a single

allResults.title: Tag Play at Third
allResults.concept: On tag plays, sweep the glove low in front of the bag to meet the sliding runner before he reaches the base.

allResults.title: Hot Corner Rocket
allResults.concept: After knocking down a hard shot, look for the closest force out — stepping on the bag beats a risky throw every time.

allResults.title: Tag the Runner at Third
allResults.concept: On headfirst slides, sweep the tag low in front of the bag — meet the runner's hands before they reach the base.

allResults.title: Bunt Defense — Charge and Throw
allResults.concept: On bunt plays, charge aggressively and throw to first for the sure out — don't try for the lead runner when your momentum is going the wrong way.

allResults.title: The Slow Roller — Charge or Wait?
allResults.concept: Slow roller: charge hard and field while clearly fair — never wait

allResults.title: The Bunt Crash — Who Gets It?
allResults.concept: Third-base-line bunts: 3B owns it — call it loudly and early

allResults.title: Guarding the Line — When?
allResults.concept: Guard the third base line in innings 7-9 with a 1-2 run lead

allResults.title: The Tag Play at Third
allResults.concept: Force at 2nd removed = tag play at third, not force

allResults.title: Third Base Leads — Coaching the Runner
allResults.concept: As cutoff at third: read the runner and decide to cut or let it through

allResults.title: Tag Up Deep Fly
allResults.concept: Always tag up on deep fly balls from 3rd with less than 2 outs

allResults.title: Two Outs, Run on Contact
allResults.concept: With 2 outs, ALWAYS run on contact — nothing to lose

allResults.title: Running Through First Base
allResults.concept: Run full speed through 1st base — you can overrun it safely in foul territory

allResults.title: Left-Handed Pitcher on the Mound
allResults.concept: Steal on lefties by reading the front foot — if it crosses toward home, GO!

allResults.title: Deep Fly Ball — Tag and Score
allResults.concept: On a deep fly ball with less than 2 outs, tag up — touch the base and go when the ball is caught

allResults.title: Shallow Fly — Too Risky?
allResults.concept: Not every fly ball is a good tag-up — shallow fly balls give the outfielder an easy throw home

allResults.title: Advancing from Second on a Fly
allResults.concept: Tag from 2nd to 3rd on deep flies to right field — the right fielder has the longest throw to 3rd

allResults.title: Line Drive — Freeze!
allResults.concept: On line drives, freeze and get back to your base — if it's caught, you can't get doubled off

allResults.title: Sacrifice Fly Timing
allResults.concept: On a tag-up, leave the moment the ball touches the glove — not before, not after

allResults.title: Foul Ball Tag-Up
allResults.concept: You CAN tag up on foul ball catches — a catch is a catch, fair or foul

allResults.title: Deep Fly to the Warning Track
allResults.concept: When the outfielder catches at the warning track, tag and score — the throw is too long to beat you

allResults.title: Line Drive at the Shortstop
allResults.concept: On line drives hit at fielders, get back to your base — don't get doubled off

allResults.title: Fly Ball to the Outfield — Go Halfway
allResults.concept: On fly balls, go halfway — you can advance if it drops and retreat if it's caught

allResults.title: Sharp Grounder to Third — Frozen at Second
allResults.concept: When the third baseman fields near his bag, stay at second — don't run into a tag

allResults.title: Hard Grounder Back to the Pitcher
allResults.concept: When the pitcher fakes a throw home, freeze — don't fall for the oldest trick in the book

allResults.title: Passed Ball — Score from Third
allResults.concept: On passed balls from 3rd base, sprint home — react instantly, don't watch and wait

allResults.title: Balk — Free Base
allResults.concept: On a balk, all runners advance one base — it's a free base when the pitcher makes an illegal motion

allResults.title: Rundown — Make Them Throw
allResults.concept: In a rundown, make them throw — every throw is a chance for an error and lets other runners advance

allResults.title: Ground Rule Double — Where Do I Go?
allResults.concept: On a ground rule double, all runners advance exactly two bases from where they started

allResults.title: Obstruction — Fielder in My Way
allResults.concept: If a fielder blocks you without the ball, it's obstruction — keep running, the umpire will award the base

allResults.title: Appeal Play — Missed a Base
allResults.concept: If you miss a base, go back and touch it before the defense appeals — fix your mistake yourself

allResults.title: Force Removed — Am I Still Forced?
allResults.concept: When the runner behind you is forced out, your force is removed — you must be tagged, not just forced

allResults.title: The Double Steal — Your Read
allResults.concept: On a double steal, the runner at second reads the catcher's body commitment — go the instant the catcher's weight shifts toward second, before the throw is released

allResults.title: First to Third — Do You Go?
allResults.concept: Ball in the RF corner with the outfielder's back to you and the coach windmilling is a go situation — sprint through second without hesitation

allResults.title: Should I Try to Steal?
allResults.concept: At the youth level, steal when the catcher is struggling to throw — you only need about a 50% success rate for stealing to help your team

allResults.title: When to Call Timeout
allResults.concept: Mound visits break momentum and refocus your pitcher — use them strategically

allResults.title: Batting Order Basics
allResults.concept: Leadoff hitter = speed + on-base ability. They set the table for the whole lineup

allResults.title: No-Hitter Through 6 — High Pitch Count
allResults.concept: Pitch count and control matter more than chasing a no-hitter — protect the arm and the win

allResults.title: Sacrifice Bunt or Swing Away?
allResults.concept: With a weak hitter and a runner to advance, the sacrifice bunt is a productive out

allResults.title: Hit and Run — When to Call It Off
allResults.concept: Never run the hit-and-run on 0-2 — the batter can't protect the runner when the pitcher owns the count

allResults.title: Slumping Cleanup Hitter
allResults.concept: Trust your best hitters through slumps — don't panic-move your lineup after a bad week

allResults.title: Late Game Defense — Sub in Glove Man?
allResults.concept: In late innings with a lead, defense matters more than offense — bring in your best glove

allResults.title: Guard the Lines in the 9th
allResults.concept: In the 9th with a lead, guard the foul lines — a double is more dangerous than a single

allResults.title: Outfielder Tired — Defensive Sub?
allResults.concept: In close games, protect center field — it's the most important defensive position in the outfield

allResults.title: Fastest Player — Where Does He Bat?
allResults.concept: Your fastest player with the best on-base skills bats leadoff — speed + OBP = leadoff hitter

allResults.title: Platoon Advantage — Who Starts?
allResults.concept: Use the platoon advantage — start the opposite-handed hitter against the opposing pitcher

allResults.title: Hot Hitter — Move Him Up?
allResults.concept: Don't rearrange the lineup for one hot week — hot streaks end, but lineup construction is based on talent

allResults.title: Too Many Lefties — Adjust?
allResults.concept: Against a tough same-side pitcher, mix in opposite-handed hitters but keep your core players

allResults.title: Extra Innings — Manage the Bullpen
allResults.concept: In extra innings, use your best available arm — win the game NOW, don't save for hypothetical later innings

allResults.title: Team Made 3 Errors — Keep Morale Up
allResults.concept: After errors, encourage your team — positive energy prevents more mistakes, negativity causes them

allResults.title: Fighting for Playoffs — Use the Ace on Short Rest?
allResults.concept: Save your ace for normal rest — a fully rested ace is more valuable than a tired one on short rest

allResults.title: Bench-Clearing Situation — Handle Conflict
allResults.concept: In heated situations, hold your team back — ejections and suspensions hurt more than the other team's pitcher

allResults.title: The Appeal Window
allResults.concept: Appeal at the base the runner LEFT FROM, not where they went — and a 3rd-out appeal erases any run scored on the same play

allResults.title: Bunt or Swing? Middle School Level
allResults.concept: At the middle school level, sacrifice bunts are more valuable than in MLB — weaker fielding makes bunts succeed more often and the RE cost is lower

allResults.title: Relay Read — Standard Play
allResults.concept: With 0 outs in a tied game, the relay home is the priority when alignment is set

allResults.title: Relay Read — Two Outs Changes Everything
allResults.concept: With 2 outs the relay home is MORE important — every run is magnified when the inning is about to end

allResults.title: Relay Read — Slow Runner Changes the Math
allResults.concept: A slow runner changes the relay decision — cut and hold to keep the double play alive rather than throwing to an empty plate

allResults.title: Relay Read — Elite Arm in Left Field
allResults.concept: Hit the cutoff man always — even elite arms work through the relay system because reads matter more than raw arm strength

allResults.title: Infield Depth — Big Lead Changes the Calculus
allResults.concept: With a big lead, normal depth prioritizes outs over individual runs — don't bring the infield in when you can afford the run

allResults.title: Steal Attempt at 2nd
allResults.concept: Quick transfer and pop time under 2.0 seconds is the key to throwing out runners

allResults.title: Pop-Up Coverage
allResults.concept: Catchers own pop-ups near home — toss mask away, turn to the infield, and take charge

allResults.title: Ball in the Dirt — Block It
allResults.concept: With runners on base, block balls in the dirt — drop to your knees and keep it in front of you

allResults.title: Wild Pitch Recovery
allResults.concept: On wild pitches, the catcher gets the ball and the pitcher covers home — it's a team play

allResults.title: Runner on Second Sees Your Signs
allResults.concept: With a runner on 2nd, use multiple signs with an indicator — single signs can be stolen

allResults.title: Passed Ball vs Wild Pitch
allResults.concept: On tough pitches, move your whole body first, then receive — don't just stab with the glove

allResults.title: Pitch Calling With Runner on Second
allResults.concept: When a runner reaches second base, switch to a secondary sign system to protect your pitch calls.

allResults.title: Blocking a Curveball in the Dirt
allResults.concept: With a runner on third, blocking is more important than catching — drop to your knees and keep the ball in front of you.

allResults.title: Pop Fly Behind the Plate
allResults.concept: On pop flies behind the plate, toss your mask away from your path, turn your back to the field, and take charge of the catch.

allResults.title: Framing a Borderline Pitch
allResults.concept: In high-leverage moments, stillness beats pulling — the umpire is watching your glove closely, and quiet presentation is more convincing.

allResults.title: Defending a Delayed Steal
allResults.concept: Delayed steals catch catchers off guard — stay alert after every pitch and use your voice to direct teammates.

allResults.title: Bunt Play With Runner on First
allResults.concept: On bunt plays, the catcher is the quarterback — get your mask off, read the play, and tell your teammates where to throw.

allResults.title: Ball in the Dirt, Strike Three!
allResults.concept: With 2 outs and first base empty, a dropped third strike lets the batter run — always recover the ball and throw to first.

allResults.title: Tag Play at Home Plate
allResults.concept: Give the runner a lane to the plate, catch the throw, and apply a swipe tag — blocking without the ball is obstruction

allResults.title: Runner Stealing Third Base
allResults.concept: Don't concede third base — a strong throw to the shortstop side of the bag can catch a runner stealing third

allResults.title: Snap Throw to Third Base
allResults.concept: A catcher snap throw to third catches runners who wander off the bag — pop up quickly and throw to the inside of the base

allResults.title: Catching a Knuckleball Pitcher
allResults.concept: Catching knuckleballs requires an oversized mitt and soft hands — let the ball come to you and react late, not early

allResults.title: Pitcher Tipping His Pitches
allResults.concept: Catchers must watch for pitch-tipping — visit the mound and check glove position, hand movement, and delivery tempo for tells

allResults.title: Jeter's Flip Play
allResults.concept: Great plays happen when you anticipate — Jeter's hustle and instinct created the impossible

allResults.title: Merkle's Boner
allResults.concept: Always touch the next base — Merkle's mistake in 1908 changed a pennant race

allResults.title: The Hidden Ball Trick
allResults.concept: The hidden ball trick only works if the pitcher is OFF the rubber — otherwise it's a balk

allResults.title: The Suicide Squeeze
allResults.concept: On a suicide squeeze, bunt EVERYTHING — the runner is committed to scoring

allResults.title: Walk-Off Balk
allResults.concept: A balk advances all runners one base — with bases loaded, that's a run

allResults.title: Bonds — Intentional Walk with Bases Loaded
allResults.concept: Sometimes giving up 1 run to avoid giving up 4 is the smart play — think expected value

allResults.title: The Catch
allResults.concept: Elite outfielders trust their speed and instincts to make plays that seem impossible — never give up on a fly ball.

allResults.title: Buckner's Grounder
allResults.concept: In pressure moments, trust the fundamentals — stay low, glove on the ground, field it clean. Routine plays win championships.

allResults.title: Gibson's Moment
allResults.concept: Great hitters study the pitcher's tendencies. Kirk Gibson knew what pitch was coming and was ready for it — preparation is a superpower.

allResults.title: Koufax's Decision
allResults.concept: Standing up for your beliefs takes courage. Sandy Koufax showed the world that character matters more than any game.

allResults.title: Carter's Walk-Off
allResults.concept: In the biggest moments, study the pitcher's weaknesses and be ready to attack. Joe Carter's World Series walk-off was all about knowing what was coming.

allResults.title: Clemente's 3000th
allResults.concept: Roberto Clemente played baseball with pride and purpose, and used his fame to help others. He showed that being a great person matters more than being a great player.

allResults.title: Shot Heard Round the World
allResults.concept: In baseball's biggest moments, the greatest players look to drive the ball. Bobby Thomson's homer is proof that aggressive confidence can produce miracles.

allResults.title: Merkle's Boner — 1908
allResults.concept: Touch every base — Merkle's failure to touch 2nd in 1908 cost the Giants the pennant and established that force-play appeals erase runs scored on the same play

allResults.title: What's a Balk?
allResults.concept: Any start-and-stop motion by the pitcher with runners on base is a balk — one free base

allResults.title: Dropped Third Strike
allResults.concept: Dropped third strike: with 2 outs or 1st base empty, the batter can run to first

allResults.title: Appeal Play — Missing a Base
allResults.concept: The defense can appeal a missed base — always touch every bag, even in the heat of the play

allResults.title: Obstruction vs Interference
allResults.concept: Obstruction: fielders can't block the baseline without the ball — runners get the base

allResults.title: Ground Rule Double
allResults.concept: Ground rule double: all runners advance exactly two bases — even if they could have scored

allResults.title: Tag Up on a Fly Ball
allResults.concept: Tag up: on a fly ball, touch your base until the catch is made, THEN you can advance

allResults.title: Infield Fly Rule
allResults.concept: The infield fly rule protects runners from being tricked. When called, the batter is automatically out even if the fielder drops the ball on purpose.

allResults.title: Tagging on Fouls
allResults.concept: Runners can tag up and advance on ANY caught fly ball — fair or foul. A deep foul ball catch can turn into a sacrifice fly!

allResults.title: Ball Hits a Runner
allResults.concept: If a batted ball hits a runner before passing a fielder, the runner is out and the ball is dead. The batter gets first base.

allResults.title: Dropped Third Strike
allResults.concept: On a dropped third strike, the batter can run to first if it's unoccupied or there are two outs. Never walk away — always run!

allResults.title: What's a Balk?
allResults.concept: A balk is an illegal motion by the pitcher. When called, all runners advance one base for free. Pitchers must follow the rules of their windup!

allResults.title: Tag Up on Foul Fly
allResults.concept: You can tag up on ANY caught fly ball — fair or foul! Smart baserunners are always ready to advance after a catch.

allResults.title: The Appeal Play
allResults.concept: An appeal play means the defense throws to the base and asks the umpire to rule. Umpires don't call tag-up violations unless the defense appeals!

allResults.title: Running the Baseline
allResults.concept: Runners must stay within three feet of the baseline when a fielder is trying to tag them. Going outside that line means you're out!

allResults.title: Ground Rule Double
allResults.concept: A ground rule double happens when a fair ball bounces over the outfield wall. The batter gets second base and all runners advance two bases.

allResults.title: Two Runners, One Base
allResults.concept: When two runners are on the same base, the lead runner (the one originally ahead) gets the base. The other runner can be tagged out!

allResults.title: Is He Out? The Third Strike Rule
allResults.concept: With 2 outs and first base empty, a dropped/uncaught third strike lets the batter run to first — the play is live and the catcher must throw him out.

allResults.title: Batting Out of Order — Who's Really Out?
allResults.concept: Batting out of order: the PROPER batter (who should have batted) is declared out, not the one who actually batted. Appeal must come before the next pitch.

allResults.title: Runner's Lane Interference — The Last 45 Feet
allResults.concept: The runner's lane rule: batter-runners must use the 3-foot dirt lane for the last 45 feet to first base or risk interference if a throw is affected.

allResults.title: Time Play vs. Force Play — Does the Run Count?
allResults.concept: The force play exception: when the third out of an inning is a force out, NO run can score on that play — even if the runner crossed home plate before the out was recorded.

allResults.title: Pitch Clock Violation — Who Gets Penalized?
allResults.concept: Pitch clock violations: pitcher too slow = automatic ball, batter not ready = automatic strike. No warnings — the penalty is immediate.

allResults.title: Fan Interference — Home Run or Dead Ball?
allResults.concept: Fan interference: when a spectator reaches INTO the field of play, it's a dead ball. The umpire places runners where they would have been. The boundary is the top of the wall.

allResults.title: Pitch Clock — Batter Not Ready
allResults.concept: Pitch clock violations: batters must be alert with 8 seconds left or it's an automatic strike

allResults.title: Pitch Clock — Strategic Step-Off
allResults.concept: Pitchers get 2 disengagements per plate appearance. A third is an automatic balk unless the runner is picked off.

allResults.title: Pitch Clock — Mound Visit Resets the Clock
allResults.concept: Mound visits reset the pitch clock to its full amount — a strategic benefit of visiting the mound

allResults.title: Infield Fly — Does It Apply with Two Outs?
allResults.concept: Infield fly rule only applies with less than 2 outs. With 2 outs, the defense has no reason to drop the ball.

allResults.title: Infield Fly — Can Runners Still Advance?
allResults.concept: On an infield fly, the batter is out but the ball is LIVE. Runners can advance at their own risk — the force is removed.

allResults.title: Checked Swing — Who Makes the Call?
allResults.concept: On a checked swing, the defense can appeal to the base umpire for a second opinion — the first or third base umpire makes the final call

allResults.title: Hit by Pitch — But It Was a Strike?
allResults.concept: A pitch in the strike zone is a strike even if it hits the batter — the strike zone takes priority over hit-by-pitch rules

allResults.title: Force Play Removal — Tricky Situation
allResults.concept: When a trailing runner is forced out, the force is removed for all runners ahead — subsequent plays become tag plays, not force plays

allResults.title: Dead Ball — When Does Play Stop?
allResults.concept: On a dead ball like hit-by-pitch, runners can only advance if forced — no extra bases on hustle during a dead ball

allResults.title: Runner Leaves Early on Tag-Up
allResults.concept: On a tag-up, the defense must appeal if a runner leaves early — it's not an automatic out, and the run counts if nobody appeals

allResults.title: Runner Interference on a Double Play
allResults.concept: The slide rule: runners must make a bona fide slide at second base — going out of the base path to disrupt a double play results in two outs

allResults.title: Ghost Runner — Extra Innings Rule
allResults.concept: The automatic runner rule places the last out of the previous inning on second base in extras — bunt to third, then try to score with a sac fly

allResults.title: Behind 0-1
allResults.concept: When you fall behind 0-1, shorten your swing and focus on contact. Protect the zone and stay alive in the at bat.

allResults.title: 1-2 Two Outs Rally
allResults.concept: In a 1-2 count with the game on the line, choke up and battle. Foul off tough pitches and wait for your opportunity — never give in to the pitcher.

allResults.title: First Pitch Fastball
allResults.concept: Do your homework! Watch the pitcher from the dugout. If you know a first-pitch fastball is coming, be aggressive and ready to attack.

allResults.title: Full Count Two Outs
allResults.concept: On a full count with two outs, protect the plate! Shorten your swing and don't go down looking. Put the ball in play and make the defense work.

allResults.title: 0-2 Waste Pitch
allResults.concept: At 0-2, use a 'waste pitch' just off the plate. You can afford a ball, and if the batter chases, you get an easy out. Expand the zone!

allResults.title: 1-1 Neutral Count
allResults.concept: At 1-1, stay balanced: look fastball and adjust to off-speed. Be ready to swing at hittable pitches but don't chase out of the zone.

allResults.title: 3-2 Protect the Plate
allResults.concept: In a 3-2 count with the game on the line, widen your zone and battle. Don't let the umpire end your season — fight off every borderline pitch.

allResults.title: 3-2 Count — Trust Your Pitch
allResults.concept: At 3-2 with 2 outs, trust your pitch and your catcher. One well-located fastball can end the inning — a walk just adds a free runner.

allResults.title: 1-0 Count — Pitcher, Don't Panic
allResults.concept: A 1-0 count is not a crisis. Stay calm, trust your stuff, and throw a quality strike. The worst thing a pitcher can do is panic and groove a pitch or overthrow.

allResults.title: 2-2 Even Count With Speed on Base
allResults.concept: Don't sacrifice your pitch count to control the running game. A slide-step fastball lets you stay competitive in the at-bat while giving your catcher a chance to throw.

allResults.title: 0-1 Batter Adjustment
allResults.concept: An 0-1 count is not a crisis for the batter. Stay with your approach, look for your pitch, and don't panic. Adjustments come at two strikes, not one.

allResults.title: 2-0 Sit on Off-Speed
allResults.concept: General rules like 'sit fastball at 2-0' are starting points. Great hitters adjust to what the specific pitcher is showing them. At 2-0, you can afford to gamble because even a wrong guess leaves you ahead.

allResults.title: 1-2 Put-Away Pitch (Pitcher's View)
allResults.concept: Elite pitch sequencing at 1-2 means changing the look, not repeating what worked. Same location, different speed catches hitters who are adjusting to what they just saw.

allResults.title: Full Count, Nobody On — Batter's Edge
allResults.concept: At 3-2, use the information from the entire at-bat. If a pitcher has been fastball-dominant and his secondary stuff is shaky, commit to the heater. Great hitters use evidence, not guesses.

allResults.title: First Batter of the Game
allResults.concept: Getting ahead in the count is a pitcher's biggest advantage

allResults.title: Protecting a Lead
allResults.concept: When fatigued, pitching smart (mixing speeds) beats pitching hard

allResults.title: Pitcher's Count
allResults.concept: The 0-2 curveball in the dirt is baseball's classic strikeout pitch

allResults.title: Third Time Through the Order
allResults.concept: Third time through the order, change speeds to disrupt hitter timing

allResults.title: In the Zone
allResults.concept: When you're in a groove, stay aggressive — don't overthink

allResults.title: Ahead 0-2 — Now What?
allResults.concept: On 0-2, use the advantage — waste a pitch out of the zone and make the hitter chase

allResults.title: Must-Throw-a-Strike Count
allResults.concept: On 3-1 counts, throw your most controllable pitch — a strike is more important than a strikeout

allResults.title: Two-Two Tightrope
allResults.concept: With a runner on third, strikeouts are more valuable than outs on contact

allResults.title: Expand or Attack?
allResults.concept: After getting a strike on the corner, expand the zone 2 inches further — the hitter's eye will follow

allResults.title: 0-2 to a Fastball Crusher
allResults.concept: Against fastball hitters, use 0-2 to show breaking balls — never throw what they crush

allResults.title: Runner on First — Stay Focused
allResults.concept: With a runner on first, your priority is the batter — but stay quick to the plate to keep the runner honest

allResults.title: Pickoff or Finish Him?
allResults.concept: Don't let an aggressive runner distract you from finishing a hitter in an 0-2 count — quick look, then attack

allResults.title: Opening At-Bat of the Game
allResults.concept: Start the game with a first-pitch strike — set the tone and take command early

allResults.title: Your Team Just Scored Big
allResults.concept: After your team scores big, put up a quick inning — keep the momentum going

allResults.title: Fastball's Not Working Today
allResults.concept: When your primary pitch isn't working, adapt — make your best pitch today your primary pitch

allResults.title: Runner on Second — Big Secondary Lead
allResults.concept: For aggressive secondary leads, use the daylight play — the catcher throws behind the runner after the pitch

allResults.title: Two Pickoffs Already — Try Again?
allResults.concept: After establishing the pickoff threat, vary your delivery timing — the combination controls runners

allResults.title: Runner Faking — Stay Composed
allResults.concept: When runners try to distract you, acknowledge with a look but stay focused on the batter

allResults.title: Walk to Load Bases — Force at Home
allResults.concept: Loading the bases for a force at home can be smart — force outs at the plate are much easier than tags

allResults.title: IBB Backfires — Surprise Pinch Hitter
allResults.concept: When a pinch hitter surprises you, attack — they're cold and you're warm. Throw strikes

allResults.title: Walk the 8th Hitter?
allResults.concept: Walk a weak hitter to face an even weaker one — pitchers batting are almost automatic outs

allResults.title: Bunt With Runners — Get the Lead Runner
allResults.concept: On a bunt with runners on 1st and 2nd, get the lead runner at third — it's a force out

allResults.title: Down to the Last Strike
allResults.concept: With your back against the wall, shorten up and make contact — keep the line moving

allResults.title: Trailing by One, Runner in Scoring Position
allResults.concept: With a hitter's count against a wild pitcher, be patient — wait for your pitch and drive it

allResults.title: The 3-0 Take
allResults.concept: At 3-0, take the pitch — a walk is almost certain and you're ahead no matter what

allResults.title: 3-1 Double Play Danger
allResults.concept: On 3-1 with a runner on 1st, look for a pitch you can lift — avoid the double play grounder

allResults.title: 2-2 — Protect or Attack?
allResults.concept: On 2-2, look for a fastball and react to off-speed — balance aggression with discipline

allResults.title: Fake Bunt and Slash
allResults.concept: The slash play exploits a charging infield — show bunt, then swing at a good pitch through the gaps

allResults.title: Two Strikes — Breaking Ball Barrage
allResults.concept: After a breaking ball barrage, stay reactive — don't guess, let your eyes and hands adjust

allResults.title: Choking Up — Why and When
allResults.concept: When you're late on fastballs, choke up — a shorter bat is a faster bat. Trade power for contact

allResults.title: Two Strikes, Runner on Third — Contact At All Costs?
allResults.concept: With the winning run on 3rd and 2 strikes, make contact — any contact is better than a strikeout

allResults.title: In a Slump — What to Adjust
allResults.concept: In a slump, simplify — look for one pitch in one zone. Rebuild confidence with quality swings

allResults.title: Bases Loaded, Cleanup Spot — Smart Approach
allResults.concept: Bases loaded, don't swing for the grand slam — hard contact with the bases loaded is devastating enough

allResults.title: Third At-Bat — You've Seen Everything
allResults.concept: Third at-bat, use your data — expect fastball early, react to off-speed with what you've learned

allResults.title: Double Play Chance
allResults.concept: When a ground ball comes right to you, turn the double play yourself

allResults.title: Runner on Second — Hold or Play Back?
allResults.concept: With only a runner on 2nd, play normal depth — no conventional DP is available without a runner on first

allResults.title: Pop Fly in Shallow Right
allResults.concept: Outfielders coming in have priority over infielders going back — with a runner on third, the outfielder's forward momentum also sets up a better throw home on a tag-up.

allResults.title: Double Play Feed From the Pitcher
allResults.concept: On a double play feed from the pitcher, touch second with your left foot and redirect a quick sidearm throw to first — footwork and momentum are the keys.

allResults.title: Pop Fly — You or the Right Fielder?
allResults.concept: Outfielders charging in always have priority over infielders going back — the ball is easier to catch running forward, and with a runner on third, the outfielder has better throwing momentum toward home.

allResults.title: Ground Ball to Short — Runner on First
allResults.concept: With a runner on first and nobody out, the double play is always the first thought

allResults.title: Cutoff Man Position on a Relay
allResults.concept: As the relay man, line up between the outfielder and home plate — give the outfielder a clear target

allResults.title: Relay on a Left-Center Gap Shot
allResults.concept: On relay plays to left-center, the shortstop lines up between the outfielder and home plate — preventing runs is always the default priority. Hands up, ears open.

allResults.title: Double Play Pivot — Inside or Outside?
allResults.concept: Double play pivot: inside vs. outside depends on throw direction

allResults.title: Fly Ball Caught — Throw Home or Cutoff?
allResults.concept: On tag-up plays, throw directly home — speed matters more than accuracy on this play

allResults.title: Take Charge on the Pop Fly
allResults.concept: The center fielder is the outfield captain and has priority on any fly ball they can reach.

allResults.title: Shade the Gap
allResults.concept: Study the batter's stance and tendencies to shade your position toward where they're most likely to hit.

allResults.title: Hit the Right Base
allResults.concept: Always target the lead runner — getting the out on the most advanced baserunner takes away the biggest scoring threat.

allResults.title: Go Back on the Deep Ball
allResults.concept: On balls over your head, drop-step to the side the ball is drifting, sprint at an angle, and track it over your shoulder.

allResults.title: Shallow or Deep?
allResults.concept: In late-game situations with a runner on third, play shallow to give yourself a shorter throw home and a chance to save the run.

allResults.title: Backing Up Second
allResults.concept: Center fielders back up second base on every steal attempt and infield throw — sprint in directly behind the bag, in line with the throw.

allResults.title: Catchable or Uncatchable — The Split-Second Decision
allResults.concept: Use the 80% rule on dive decisions: dive if you're 80%+ confident, play the carom below that. The threshold changes with outs and game situation.

allResults.title: Directing the Outfield — You're the Captain
allResults.concept: Center fielder is the outfield captain — direct both corner outfielders into position before every pitch based on the hitter and situation

allResults.title: Hit the Cutoff
allResults.concept: Always hit the cutoff man — he makes the best decision for the team

allResults.title: Ball in the Sun
allResults.concept: Use your glove to shade the sun — fundamental outfield technique

allResults.title: Ball Off the Wall
allResults.concept: Round the ball off the wall so your momentum is already heading toward the infield — never field and spin.

allResults.title: Shallow Fly Communication
allResults.concept: Outfielders have priority on shallow flies because they're running forward with the ball in front of them — call it loud and early.

allResults.title: Sun in Your Eyes
allResults.concept: In the sun, use sunglasses AND the glove-shield technique — sunglasses are primary, glove-up is your always-available backup.

allResults.title: Sac Fly Positioning
allResults.concept: With a runner on third and fewer than two outs, move in a few steps — shorten your throw home without giving up catchable fly balls.

allResults.title: Reading the Bat Angle — Pull or Opposite
allResults.concept: Trust pull-rate data over single at-bat stance changes — a 70% pull hitter pulls even more on hitter's counts

allResults.title: Do-or-Die — Runner Tagging from Third
allResults.concept: With an average arm on a do-or-die play, pre-position to catch moving toward the infield and use the cutoff relay

allResults.title: First Base Fundamentals
allResults.concept: First baseman: get to the bag early, face the thrower, stretch toward the throw

allResults.title: Let It Through or Cut It Off?
allResults.concept: As the cutoff man, listen to the catcher — he can see the play and tells you to cut or let it go

allResults.title: Pull Hitter — Shade or Stay?
allResults.concept: Against pull hitters, shade the infield toward the pull side — position where the ball is going

allResults.title: Holding the Runner On
allResults.concept: With a fast runner on first, the first baseman's primary job is to hold the runner on by staying on the bag and giving the pitcher a pickoff target.

allResults.title: Fielding a Bunt as the First Baseman
allResults.concept: On bunt plays, the first baseman fields balls in his zone and flips to the second baseman covering first for the sure out.

allResults.title: Cover First or Play the Ball?
allResults.concept: First basemen must decide instantly: go after the ball or get back to the bag. When the second baseman has a better angle, get to first and be the target.

allResults.title: 3-6-3 Double Play — Quick Feet
allResults.concept: On the 3-6-3 double play, always throw to second FIRST to keep the force play alive — stepping on first first kills the force at second.

allResults.title: Ball at the Wall — Make the Play or Protect Yourself?
allResults.concept: At the wall, use your free hand to find and brace against it while catching with your glove hand

allResults.title: Throw Through or Hit Cutoff
allResults.concept: When in doubt, hit the cutoff man — it keeps every option alive and prevents the throw from becoming an error that gives up extra bases.

allResults.title: Ball Down the Right Field Line
allResults.concept: On balls down the right field line, angle your approach so you're already facing the infield when you field the ball — it saves a full turn on your throw.

allResults.title: Fly Ball With Wind Blowing In
allResults.concept: When the wind blows in, fly balls lose distance — adjust your positioning a few steps toward the infield to catch the ball where it actually lands.

allResults.title: Throwing on the Run vs. Setting Your Feet
allResults.concept: Right fielders must set their feet for the long throw to third — accuracy beats speed on this distance.

allResults.title: Do-or-Die — Runner Tagging from Second on a Fly
allResults.concept: When a runner tags from second to third with one out in a close game, the right fielder MUST challenge the throw — third base with one out is nearly automatic scoring position.

allResults.title: Power Hitter — Deep or Guard the Line?
allResults.concept: In the 9th with a lead, guard the line — a double is more dangerous than a single

allResults.title: Bare-Hand Roller
allResults.concept: On dying rollers, bare-handing saves the glove transfer time needed to beat fast runners on the long throw to first.

allResults.title: Long Throw Mechanics
allResults.concept: The crow-hop builds momentum for the long throw across the diamond — your body generates the power, not just your arm.

allResults.title: Guard the Line
allResults.concept: In late innings with a lead, guard the line to prevent extra-base hits — a single hurts less than a double.

allResults.title: Foul Ball Near Dugout
allResults.concept: On foul pops, know your surroundings — catch the ball confidently, but never blindly run into the dugout.

allResults.title: Hot Corner Line Drive
allResults.concept: At the hot corner, keep your glove centered and ready on every pitch — react with soft hands and let the ball hit the glove rather than stabbing at it.

allResults.title: Stealing Second
allResults.concept: Steal when conditions align: slow pitcher, weak catcher, hitter's count

allResults.title: Scoring from 2nd
allResults.concept: Round 3rd aggressively and read your coach — he sees the whole play

allResults.title: Slow Pitcher, Big Lead
allResults.concept: A pitcher's delivery time is the key to stealing bases — slow deliveries mean easy steals

allResults.title: The Delayed Steal
allResults.concept: The delayed steal exploits a lazy catcher — go when they lob the ball back to the pitcher

allResults.title: Double Steal — First and Third
allResults.concept: On a double steal from 1st and 3rd, force the catcher to choose — throw to 2nd and the runner on 3rd scores

allResults.title: Two Runners Tagging
allResults.concept: When two runners can tag, both should go — the defense can only make one throw

allResults.title: Ground Ball to the Left Side
allResults.concept: On a ground ball with a runner on first, you MUST advance — it's a force play

allResults.title: Ground Ball to the Right Side — Second to Third

allResults.concept: If you suspect sign stealing, change your signs immediately — prevention is better than accusation

allResults.title: Late Game, Tie — One Run or Big Inning?
allResults.concept: In a tie game, play for a big inning — one run can be matched. Multiple runs end the game

allResults.title: Day Game After Night Game
allResults.concept: After a night game, rest 2-3 key players — strategic rest over a season prevents injuries and burnout

allResults.title: Infield In — Cut Off the Run
allResults.concept: With a runner on 3rd in a tie game, bring the infield in — preventing the run is the priority

allResults.title: The Leverage Index Trap
allResults.concept: Leverage index determines WHEN to use your best reliever — a tie 8th inning (1.5 LI) with 3rd TTO is the moment to go to your setup man

allResults.title: The TTO Compound Alarm
allResults.concept: TTO effect (+30 BA pts) COMPOUNDS with platoon disadvantage (+18 pts) — a RHP facing LHH for the 3rd time faces a +48 point BA spike

allResults.title: Turf Speed — Adjust Your Depth
allResults.concept: On artificial turf, ground balls travel 15-20% faster — infielders must play 1-2 steps deeper to compensate for the reduced reaction time

allResults.title: Bunt Call — Tied Game, 9th Inning, Weak Hitter
allResults.concept: RE24 measures expected runs, but in walk-off situations you need SCORING PROBABILITY — the chance of exactly one run. Bunting a weak hitter in the 9th is the right play.

allResults.title: Bunt Call — Safety Squeeze vs. Suicide Squeeze
allResults.concept: Safety squeeze: runner reads the bunt first, then breaks. Suicide squeeze: runner goes blind. Use safety with a decent hitter and 1 out.

allResults.title: Infield Depth — Close Game, Runner on 3rd
allResults.concept: Infield in with a 1-run lead and runner on 3rd — the tying run is worth more than a slightly higher hit probability

allResults.title: Win Probability — Down 1 in the 8th, Use Your Closer?
allResults.concept: Use your best pitcher in the highest-leverage moment, not just in 'save situations' — win probability doesn't care about traditional closer roles

allResults.title: Win Probability — Intentional Walk to Load the Bases?
allResults.concept: Loading the bases to set up force plays and face a weaker hitter can increase your win probability — the force at home is worth the extra baserunner

allResults.title: Pitch Calling — Full Count
allResults.concept: Use a hitter's weakness even in full counts — pattern recognition wins

allResults.title: Blocking a Pitch in the Dirt
allResults.concept: Block first, catch second — keep your body centered behind the ball in the dirt

allResults.title: Framing a Borderline Pitch
allResults.concept: Elite framing is subtle — catch, stick, and gently guide toward the zone

allResults.title: Pitch Calling — Leadoff Hitter
allResults.concept: Getting strike one against leadoff hitters sets the tone for the entire inning

allResults.title: Squeeze Play Defense
allResults.concept: The best squeeze defense is pitching high and inside — it's nearly impossible to bunt

allResults.title: Pitch Calling — Power Hitter
allResults.concept: Pitch away from a pull hitter's strength — make them beat you the opposite way

allResults.title: Wild Pitch — Ball to the Backstop
allResults.concept: On a wild pitch, go to the ball FIRST — you can't make any play without the ball in your hand

allResults.title: Communication — Positioning
allResults.concept: The catcher is the field general — direct defensive positioning from behind the plate

allResults.title: Passed Ball Prevention
allResults.concept: Anticipate the block before the pitch — pre-shift weight and be ready for dirt

allResults.title: Batter Keeps Fouling Off Pitches
allResults.concept: When a batter is fouling off your fastball, change speeds — a changeup disrupts his timing

allResults.title: Mound Visit — Pitcher Losing Control
allResults.concept: On mound visits, lighten the mood first, then give a clear plan — frustrated pitchers need a reset

allResults.title: Batter Peeks at Your Signs
allResults.concept: If a batter peeks at your signs, show one pitch and call another — use his cheating against him

allResults.title: Pitcher Wants Fastball, You See Danger
allResults.concept: When you disagree with your pitcher, call a mound visit — great catchers communicate, not just receive

allResults.title: Pickoff Play at First Base
allResults.concept: Watch runners' habits after every pitch — a quick snap throw from the catcher can catch a runner with a lazy secondary lead.

allResults.title: Runner on First Complicates It
allResults.concept: With a runner on first and fewer than 2 outs, the dropped third strike rule does NOT apply — the batter is out to prevent easy double plays.

allResults.title: Intentional Walk Setup
allResults.concept: The automatic intentional walk rule lets you skip the four pitches — the manager signals the umpire and the batter takes first base

allResults.title: Setting Up the Double Play
allResults.concept: To set up a double play, call pitches low in the zone that produce ground balls — sinkers and two-seamers to the pull side are ideal

allResults.title: Baez's Rundown Magic
allResults.concept: In a rundown, make them throw — every throw is a chance for an error

allResults.title: Three Men on Third
allResults.concept: The lead runner has the right to the base — tag the others, they're trespassing

allResults.title: Jackie Steals Home
allResults.concept: Jackie Robinson played baseball with fearless courage — the same courage that made him a pioneer who changed the game forever.

allResults.title: Infield Fly — Foul Territory Twist
allResults.concept: 'Infield Fly If Fair' is a conditional call. If caught in foul territory, the infield fly doesn't apply — but the catch still retires the batter.

allResults.title: 0-0: First Pitch Approach (Batter)
allResults.concept: Hitting 0-0: if you know what's coming, be aggressive — hitters bat .340 on first pitches

allResults.title: 1-2: Pitcher's Strategy
allResults.concept: 1-2 counts: expand the zone with chase pitches just off the plate — make the hitter swing at balls

allResults.title: 2-0: Pitcher in Trouble
allResults.concept: Behind 2-0, throw your best fastball for a strike — command beats trickery when you're behind

allResults.title: First Pitch After a Walk (Pitcher)
allResults.concept: After a walk, throw strike one immediately — getting ahead prevents walk snowballs

allResults.title: Count-Based Stealing
allResults.concept: The best counts for stealing are when the pitcher is slightly behind (1-0, 2-1) because he's focused on throwing a strike to the batter and more likely to throw a predictable fastball.
